macro_segments.title: The Debate Between Rabba and Rava: Reasons for Saying "Bifnei Nichtav U'Bifnei Nechtam"

micro_segments.title: Overview of the Rabba-Rava Debate and Context from Previous Study
The Case of Asasyos: Two Governments in One City and the Explanation of Eretz Yisrael's Special Status
Evaluation of Evidence: Rabba Concedes to Rava Regarding Witness Validation
Distinguishing Between the Two Concerns: Bifnei Nichtav (writing) and Bifnei Nechtam (signing)
The Remaining Debate After Concession: Cases Without Signature Validation Concerns
Two Witnesses Bringing the Get: Implicit Validation Without Needing the Declaration
Cases Where Lishmah Concerns Remain: Chutz La'aretz Without Signature Validation

macro_segments.title: The Mute or Uninformed Messenger: Aino Yachol Lomar and the Role of Signature Validation

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Case: Mishna's Statement on the Deaf-Mute and the Confusion Over Aino Yachol
Rav Yosef's Explanation: Healthy Person Who Became Deaf-Mute After Delivery
Tosafot's Alternative: Why Not Simply a Mute or Someone Who Didn't Witness?
The Clear Solution: Validation of Signatures as the Primary Concern

macro_segments.title: Reconciling the Evidence: The Shift from Lishmah to Signature Validation After Learning

micro_segments.title: The Problem: Mishna's Language Suggests Lishmah Concerns, But Evidence Points to Signatures
Gezeirah Shema Yachzor: Maintaining the Original Practice Despite Changed Circumstances
Distinguishing Lechatachila from Bedi'eved: When the Requirement Applies vs. When Alternatives Suffice
Unusual Cases: Why Bizarre Situations Like the Deaf-Mute Don't Trigger the Gezeirah
The Woman Bringing Her Own Get: Why Lo Plug Applies Even in Abnormal Situations
Resolution: Maintaining Consistency in Normal Cases While Allowing Exceptions When No Choice Exists

macro_segments.title: The Husband Delivering His Own Get: Why the Teshuva Concerns Don't Apply

micro_segments.title: The Question: Should the Husband Also Declare Bifnei Nichtav to Address Lishmah Concerns?
The Real Concern: Protection Against Husband's Challenge, Not Actual Lishmah Issues
Why the Husband Need Not Declare: Personal Involvement Eliminates Challenge Pretext

macro_segments.title: Two Messengers Bringing the Get: Distinguishing from Standard Shlichus

micro_segments.title: Shmuel and Rav Huna's Dispute: Must Two Messengers Declare Bifnei Nichtav?
The Answer: Two Messengers Don't Need to Declare Because of Milta D'Lo Shechicha
Consistency Check: Why Woman Bringing Her Own Get Still Requires Declaration
Tosafot's Distinction: Two Messengers as Testimony vs. Shlichus Paradigm

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's Position: The Lishmah-Only View and Two Messengers Reconciliation

micro_segments.title: Proof for Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: After People Learned the Laws of Lishmah, the Requirement Ceased
The Problem: This Seems to Contradict the Earlier Conclusion That Everyone Agrees on Signatures
The Solution: Rabbi Shimon bar Abba Likely Brought the Get with a Second Messenger (Implicit Two-Messenger Case)
Why This Resolves the Apparent Contradiction: Two Messengers Are an Unusual Case Exempt from the Gezeirah

macro_segments.title: The Number of Witnesses Present for Get Delivery: Two vs. Three and the Shliach-Eid-Dayan Identity Question

micro_segments.title: The Debate: Rabbi Yochanan Holds Two Witnesses, Another View Holds Three
Proof from Rachavei bar Rav Chisda: Rabbi Yochanan Says Deliver Before Two
Initial Theory: Two Suggests Lishmah Concern, Three Suggests Signature Validation
Refutation: Rabbi Yochanan Holds Signature Validation, So Why Only Two?
Revised Theory: The Debate Concerns Shliach Na'aseh Eid vs. Whether Eid Na'aseh Dayan for Own Testimony
The Halcha: For Rabbinic Testimony, Eid Can Become Dayan, But Stringency Debate Remains
The Practical Debate: Preventing Confusion About Whether a Woman Can Serve as Dayan

macro_segments.title: Failure to Declare and Remediation: Rabbi Meir's Stringency vs. The Sages' Leniency

micro_segments.title: Accepted Practice: Two Witnesses Suffice for Delivery; Source from Braita on Regifting
Rabbi Meir's Position: Failure to Declare Invalidates the Get and Bastardizes Children
The Sages' Disagreement: Children Are Not Bastards; Remedies Exist for Lechatachila
Tosafot on Timing: When Must the Declaration Be Made (During, Immediately After, or Before)
Rabbi Meir's Rationale: Any Deviation from the Established Mold Regarding Gittin Results in Bastardization

topical_tags.term: Bifnei Nichtav U'Bifnei Nechtam (Declaration of proper writing and signing)
Lishmah (with proper intent for this specific couple)
Ein Eidim Metzuyim Lekaymo (absence of witnesses to validate signatures)
Eretz Yisrael vs. Chutz La'aretz distinction
Gezeirah Shema Yachzor Hadavar Lekilkulo (fearing conditions will revert)
Lechatachila vs. Bedi'eved (prospective vs. retroactive standards)
Lo Plug (not making distinctions between cases)
Eid Na'aseh Dayan (witness becomes judge in his own case)
Laachar Shelamdu (after people learned the laws)
Matbe'a (mold or established form for gittin)
